lead_signal.headline: Sudan SAF-RSF war enters new multi-front phase as RSF and SPLM-N advance on Blue Nile state capital Damazin while SAF targets RSF gold mines and resumes manned airstrikes in Kordofan, deepening partition risk.

conflict_updates.title: RSF and SPLM-N Advance on Blue Nile Capital Damazin; SAF Resumes Manned Airstrikes in Kordofan
conflict_updates.summary: RSF forces and the SPLM-N faction captured the Al-Keili area in Blue Nile state on 25 April and continued advancing northward toward the state capital Damazin, opening a new front bordering Ethiopia. Simultaneously, SAF-aligned forces launched an offensive across North Kordofan on 18 April aimed at securing the El Obeid–Kadugli road, and the SAF resumed manned aircraft bombing of RSF targets around Dilling on 22 April. The multi-front expansion signals a structural escalation in operational tempo rather than an episodic flare, with both sides contesting new terrain while drone strikes on civilian areas continue to intensify.

sources_cited.excerpt: RSF and SPLM-N 25 April captured Al-Keili area, advanced north toward capital Damazin
Army 22 April resumed manned aircraft operations, bombing RSF targets around Dilling
Neither side seems on verge of achieving supremacy; partition risk growing

research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: Two independent Tier 1 sources (ICG CrisisWatch and ACLED Sudan page) confirm the Blue Nile advance and Kordofan offensive with consistent operational detail. Multi-source threshold met for High confidence. Drone strikes on civilians corroborated by ACLED and ICG separately.

conflict_updates.confidence_basis: Confirmed by two independent Tier 1 sources (ICG CrisisWatch April 2026 entry and ACLED Sudan country page). No US diplomatic de-weighting applicable to this military posture finding.

cross_monitor_relevance.wdm: SAF targeting of RSF-controlled gold mines in South Darfur-South Sudan borderlands has resource-extraction governance implications for any post-conflict political settlement.
cross_monitor_relevance.erm: Blue Nile front expansion threatens agricultural zones and water infrastructure in one of Sudan's most food-insecure regions; displacement acceleration likely.
cross_monitor_relevance.gmm: SAF strikes on RSF-controlled gold mines in South Darfur-South Sudan borderlands constitute direct economic warfare targeting illicit revenue streams that fund RSF operations.
cross_monitor_relevance.fcw: ACLED notes Middle East tensions are reshaping Sudan's war economy and supply networks; Iranian war distraction effect on Gulf mediators documented as reducing diplomatic bandwidth for Sudan.

conflict_updates.title: SAF Strikes RSF-Controlled Gold Mines in South Darfur-South Sudan Borderlands
conflict_updates.summary: In late April 2026, the SAF began targeting RSF-controlled gold mines in the South Darfur–South Sudan borderlands as part of coordinated strikes announced on 23 April aimed at destroying RSF weapons depots across several states. This represents a deliberate economic warfare dimension: gold mining revenue is a primary RSF funding mechanism, and its disruption is intended to degrade RSF operational sustainability. The strikes also risk cross-border escalation given the proximity to South Sudan, where RSF-aligned networks operate.

sources_cited.excerpt: Late April began targeting RSF-controlled gold mines in South Darfur-South Sudan borderlands
Army 23 April announced coordinated strikes across several states aimed at destroying RSF weapons depots

research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: Two Tier 1 sources confirm the coordinated strike campaign and gold mine targeting. Economic warfare dimension assessed as structural given RSF's documented reliance on artisanal gold revenue.

conflict_updates.confidence_basis: Two independent Tier 1 sources confirm. Economic warfare framing supported by prior ACLED reporting on RSF gold revenue dependency.

cross_monitor_relevance.erm: Destruction of artisanal mining sites displaces mining-dependent civilian populations in already fragile borderland communities.
cross_monitor_relevance.gmm: RSF gold exports via UAE networks have been a documented sanctions-evasion mechanism; SAF targeting of mines may reduce illicit gold flows but will not eliminate them given alternative smuggling routes.

conflict_updates.title: Sudan Displacement Exceeds 4 Million Refugees; Partition Risk Accelerates Internal Displacement
conflict_updates.summary: Crisis Group's April 2026 briefing confirms the Sudan war has compelled as many as 4 million Sudanese to flee the country, with many more internally displaced, and several areas in prolonged hunger or famine. The risk of long-term partition is growing as both sides have named governments staking claims to nationwide sovereignty. The Blue Nile front expansion and Kordofan offensive are expected to generate new displacement waves in areas with limited humanitarian access.
conflict_updates.source_name: Crisis Group Africa Briefing N°211 — Divided Sudan, Elusive Peace

sources_cited.excerpt: War has compelled as many as four million Sudanese to flee the country
Drone warfare also continued, increasingly striking civilian areas

research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: Displacement figures from Crisis Group April 2026 briefing. IDMC direct data not retrieved this cycle due to search limit constraints; Crisis Group figures are consistent with prior IDMC baselines. Flagged as coverage gap.

conflict_updates.confidence_basis: Crisis Group April 2026 briefing provides current displacement figures. IDMC direct query not completed this cycle — flagged as partial coverage gap. Figures consistent with prior IDMC reporting trajectory.

cross_monitor_relevance.erm: Mass displacement into Chad and South Sudan is generating secondary climate-conflict stress in already fragile Sahelian receiving communities.
cross_monitor_relevance.gmm: Famine conditions in multiple Sudanese states are creating acute food security market disruptions with regional commodity implications.
cross_monitor_relevance.fcw: Sudan's conflict remains systematically underreported in international media per ACLED and ICG; information vacuum creates conditions for FIMI exploitation of the narrative space.

conflict_updates.title: Russia Resumes Mass Strikes After Orthodox Easter Ceasefire; Advances in Sumy, Kharkiv, Donetsk, and Zaporizhia
conflict_updates.summary: Following a short Easter ceasefire (11–12 April), Russia resumed large-scale strikes on Ukrainian cities, killing at least 58 civilians across multiple oblasts in the week of 11–17 April. Russian forces simultaneously advanced along the international border in Sumy region, captured two settlements near Vovchansk in Kharkiv, made advances near Pokrovsk in Donetsk, and seized a village near Huliaipole in Zaporizhia. ACLED analysis assesses Russia is using intense strike bursts to weaken Kyiv's negotiating position and internal coherence ahead of summer diplomatic pressure.

sources_cited.excerpt: Russian strikes killed at least 58 civilians in Dnipropetrovsk, Odesa, Kherson, Kharkiv, Zaporizhia, Sumy, Donetsk
Russia resumes strikes on Ukrainian cities after a short ceasefire over Orthodox Easter
Ukraine scrambles to supply air defenses as large-scale Russia attacks kill 16

Multi-source threshold met. Note: most recent ACLED weekly covers 11–17 April; the current reporting window (28 April–4 May) is partially covered by ACLED's archive page headlines but full weekly data not yet published at time of retrieval.

conflict_updates.confidence_basis: ACLED Tier 1 weekly update confirmed by AP Tier 2 reporting. Territorial advances corroborated across multiple ACLED entries. Standard drone operations not scored as I2 escalation per baseline calibration.

cross_monitor_relevance.wdm: Corruption investigations prompting departure of Zelenskyy's chief of staff Yermak noted by ICG as undermining Ukrainian institutional coherence during active conflict — democratic integrity signal.
cross_monitor_relevance.erm: Russian strikes on Dniester Hydropower Complex documented as polluting Moldova's water supply — cross-border environmental warfare dimension.
cross_monitor_relevance.gmm: Continued Russian strikes on Ukrainian energy infrastructure maintain upward pressure on European energy markets and reconstruction financing requirements.
cross_monitor_relevance.fcw: ACLED documents Russian sabotage recruitment via Telegram targeting Ukrainian civilians — active FIMI-adjacent covert influence operation in theatre.

conflict_updates.title: Easter Ceasefire Observed at Reduced Rate; Diplomatic Stalemate Persists as Russia Uses Pause to Regroup
conflict_updates.summary: Putin ordered a ceasefire from 16:00 on 11 April to midnight on 12 April following Zelenskyy's call for an Orthodox Easter truce. ACLED data show armed clashes decreased by 36% on Easter Sunday compared to the three-month daily average, but the ceasefire was loosely observed near the front line. ACLED assesses the ceasefire was used by Russia to conserve weapons for subsequent large-scale strikes — consistent with the pattern from Easter 2025. No structural diplomatic progress toward a negotiated settlement was recorded; ICG notes US-brokered diplomacy remains stained by confusion and mutual suspicion.

sources_cited.excerpt: Number of armed clashes decreased by 36% on Easter Sunday compared to daily average

sources_cited.source_name: International Crisis Group 10 Conflicts to Watch 2026

US diplomatic de-weighting applied: any US-brokered ceasefire proposals carry reduced I5 weight per schema calibration. Episodic flag applied to the ceasefire event itself as a one-off tactical pause without trajectory signal.

conflict_updates.confidence_basis: Two independent Tier 1 sources. US diplomatic de-weighting applied — US-brokered proposals assessed at reduced I5 weight. Ceasefire assessed as tactical pause, not structural diplomatic progress.
conflict_updates.episodic_reason: Easter ceasefire is a one-off tactical pause consistent with prior-year pattern; ACLED assesses it was used for Russian weapons conservation rather than as a genuine diplomatic signal. No trajectory change.

cross_monitor_relevance.fcw: ICG notes politically motivated leaks and confusion surrounding US-Russia-Ukraine diplomacy — information environment manipulation consistent with FIMI operations in the diplomatic space.

conflict_updates.title: Ukraine Conducts One of Largest Drone Swarms on Moscow in March 2026 — Assessed as Doctrine Shift
conflict_updates.summary: During 14–20 March 2026, Ukrainian forces conducted one of the largest drone assaults on Moscow since the start of the full-scale invasion, with approximately 250 drones destroyed by Russian air defenses over two days and Moscow airports shut down. ACLED records an estimated 250–300 drones entering Russian airspace in a single night on 17–18 March — one of the largest single swarms of the war. This qualifies for I2 scoring under the drone baseline calibration rule as it represents confirmed swarm doctrine first use at this scale and range, not standard drone operations.

sources_cited.excerpt: Estimated 250 to 300 drones entered Russian airspace in one of the largest single swarms of the war
Largest Drone Raid Since Full-Scale War Hits Moscow, Oil Depot Burns in Krasnodar

research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: ACLED Tier 1 with Kyiv Post corroboration. Swarm size (250–300 in single night) exceeds prior documented single-night swarm records and qualifies as swarm doctrine first use at this scale per indicator calibration rules. Scored as I2 escalation, not baseline drone activity.

conflict_updates.confidence_basis: ACLED Tier 1 with secondary source corroboration. Swarm scale qualifies for I2 scoring under calibration rules — not standard drone baseline. Note: this event occurred in the prior reporting cycle (March) but is included as structural context for current trajectory assessment.

cross_monitor_relevance.gmm: Ukrainian drone strikes on Kavkaz port damaged Russia's last two Black Sea rail ferries, with direct implications for Russian logistics and commodity export capacity.

conflict_updates.title: Israel Controls Over 75% of Gaza Territory; Hamas Disarmament Standoff Deepens
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED reports Israel has expanded its territorial control to more than 75% of Gaza since the resumption of operations following the October 2025 ceasefire, with the buffer zone significantly expanded and Israel's post-ceasefire strategy shifting from quick raids to sustained territorial control. The Board of Peace is demanding Hamas disarmament, but Hamas's reluctance stems from more than ceasefire violations — it reflects the absence of a political framework providing security guarantees. Only one senior commander from Hamas's pre-7 October military council remains in command following Israeli strikes.

sources_cited.excerpt: Israel expanding its control to more than 75% of Gaza's territory
Board of Peace wants Hamas to disarm; group's reluctance stems from more than ceasefire violations
Israel in control of the rest, blocks reconstruction and keeps striking the enclave

conflict_updates.title: Gaza Humanitarian Purgatory Deepens; Population Confined to Under 25% of Territory
conflict_updates.summary: Crisis Group's January 2026 assessment — updated through April — describes Gaza's surviving population as crowded into less than half the territory, much of it reduced to rubble, with aid remaining subject to Israeli discretion. ICG's April 2026 analysis describes the situation as a 'humanitarian purgatory' with the risk that phase one of the ceasefire calcifies into a permanent partition. Those with means to leave may begin doing so in greater numbers, with serious regional consequences. The Gaza Humanitarian Foundation distribution system has been documented as creating lethal chaos at handout points.

conflict_updates.title: US-Iran Two-Week Ceasefire Agreement Reached; Regional Ripple Effects Across Lebanon, Gulf, Yemen
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED's Middle East Conflict Monitor confirms a US-Iran two-week ceasefire agreement was reached, with ACLED experts detailing impacts across Lebanon, the Gulf, Yemen, Israel, and Iraq. The ceasefire followed a period of intense Iranian missile and drone strikes against Israel (90+ attempted strikes between 28 February and 4 March alone) and Iranian retaliatory strikes on Gulf energy infrastructure including the Pearl GTL facility in Qatar. The ceasefire is assessed as fragile given Iran's documented preference for continued fighting over concession, and the two-week timeframe suggests a tactical pause rather than a structural de-escalation.

conflict_updates.title: Iran Missile Stockpile Degraded; Production Capacity Under Continuous Airstrikes
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED's March 2026 special issue documents that Iran possessed approximately 2,500 long-range ballistic missiles before the current war, with hundreds launched, destroyed, or targeted in storage facilities since hostilities began. The number of missile attacks on Israel declined on days three and four of the war compared to the first two days, indicating stockpile depletion and production constraints under continuous airstrikes. Iran's strategy appears aimed at sustaining pressure while calculating whether prolonged attacks can strain Israel's interception capacity — but faces clear operational limits.

conflict_updates.title: Myanmar Civil War Continues at High Intensity; Most Fragmented Conflict Globally with 1,200+ Armed Groups
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED's Conflict Index identifies Myanmar as the most fragmented conflict in the world, with over 1,200 distinct armed groups reported to have been involved in at least one violent event. The civil war continues at high levels with no trajectory toward resolution. Myanmar and Sudan together account for a disproportionate share of global conflict fragmentation. Specific operational data for the current week is limited due to structural media coverage gaps — this finding is flagged as a coverage-constrained assessment drawing on the most recent available ACLED index data.

conflict_updates.title: Congolese Military Targets M23 Areas Around Rubaya Mine; RSF Moves into North Darfur Signals Regional Linkage
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED's Africa Overview for March 2026 records the Congolese military targeting M23-controlled areas around the lucrative Rubaya coltan mine, indicating continued resource-driven conflict in eastern DRC. The same reporting cycle notes the RSF's movement into North Darfur, signaling a regional conflict linkage between DRC's eastern theatre and Sudan's western front through shared armed group networks and resource competition. Crisis Group's January 2026 assessment notes that a deal leaving Rwandan proxies in control of the Kivus cannot be an acceptable end point.

conflict_updates.title: Haiti Gang Violence Drives Near-Doubling of Fatalities; Ranks Among World's Most Severe Conflicts
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED's Conflict Index documents Haiti experienced a near-doubling of fatalities from political violence, with over 4,500 Haitians killed, and an increase in events targeting civilians, driving Haiti's ranking from 11th to 8th most severe conflict globally. Gang violence continues to be the primary driver, with targeted violence against civilians also increasing. Haiti ranks among the top 10 countries with the most severe violence in the world alongside Brazil, Ecuador, and Mexico.

conflict_updates.title: Sudan War Spills into Chad; JNIM Defections Spark Crackdown Across Central Sahel
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED's Africa Overview for April 2026 documents the Sudan war spilling over into Chad, representing a direct conflict regionalization event. The same reporting cycle records JNIM defections sparking a crackdown across the central Sahel, indicating internal fragmentation within the jihadist network that may temporarily reduce operational coherence but also signals governance stress in Mali and Burkina Faso. Maiduguri in Nigeria also faced simultaneous attacks from Boko Haram and ISWAP in the same period, indicating a broader Sahel-Lake Chad basin escalation arc.

displacement_watch.notes: Sudan displacement has crossed 4 million refugees externally with millions more internally displaced and multiple famine zones active — the most acute displacement crisis in the current monitoring window. Gaza's surviving population remains confined to under 25% of territory with aid subject to Israeli discretion and no reconstruction pathway. South Sudan's return to civil war has displaced an additional 280,000 people in Jonglei with nowhere to flee given Sudan's own war and Ethiopia's instability. The Sudan-Chad spillover is generating secondary displacement in an already climate-stressed Sahelian receiving environment. IDMC direct query was not completed this cycle due to search constraint — flagged as a partial coverage gap; figures drawn from Crisis Group April 2026 briefing consistent with prior IDMC trajectory data. Haiti's gang violence continues to drive acute urban displacement in Port-au-Prince. No humanitarian corridor improvements were identified across any active theatre this cycle.

cross_monitor_signals.wdm: Myanmar junta's suppression of democratic opposition across a 1,200+ armed group landscape and Israel-backed parallel governance structures in Gaza both represent acute democratic integrity failures with long-term implications for post-conflict political settlement design. South Sudan's 2026 elections are assessed as impossible to hold credibly given the return to civil war.
cross_monitor_signals.erm: Sudan-Chad spillover is generating cross-border displacement into climate-stressed Sahelian communities. Gaza's unexploded ordnance contamination of agricultural land represents a multi-decade environmental and food security crisis. DRC's Rubaya coltan mine conflict drives deforestation and displacement in one of the world's most biodiverse regions. Russian strikes on Ukraine's Dniester Hydropower Complex have documented cross-border environmental impacts on Moldova's water supply.
cross_monitor_signals.gmm: Iran-Gulf conflict period produced documented GDP contraction projections for Qatar (up to 14%), Kuwait (up to 14%), Saudi Arabia (~3%), and UAE (~5%); the US-Iran ceasefire partially arrests this trajectory but structural energy market vulnerability persists. RSF gold mine targeting by SAF in Sudan disrupts illicit gold export networks routed through UAE. DRC's Rubaya coltan mine under M23 control represents a strategic commodity disruption in global electronics supply chains. Russia's continued strikes on Ukrainian energy infrastructure maintain upward pressure on European energy markets.
cross_monitor_signals.fcw: Russian Telegram-based recruitment of Ukrainian civilians for IED attacks represents an active FIMI-adjacent covert influence operation. Houthi framing of US-Iran ceasefire as 'historical victory for the Axis of Resistance' is a documented information operation. DRC and Myanmar information vacuums are being exploited by Rwandan government narrative operations and Myanmar junta media control respectively. Sudan's systematic underreporting in international media — documented by ACLED and ICG — creates conditions for FIMI exploitation of the narrative space, particularly given Gulf mediator distraction by the Iran war.

weekly_brief_narrative:
The dominant signal of the current reporting cycle is Sudan's accelerating multi-front war, which has entered a new operational phase characterized by simultaneous SAF offensives in Kordofan, RSF-SPLM-N advances on Blue Nile state capital Damazin, and SAF targeting of RSF-controlled gold mines in the South Darfur-South Sudan borderlands. The Blue Nile front opening is particularly significant: it borders Ethiopia, introduces a new geographic axis into a war already spanning Darfur, Kordofan, and Khartoum, and threatens to draw in additional regional actors. Crisis Group's April 2026 briefing confirms the partition risk is growing as both sides have named governments staking claims to nationwide sovereignty. With 4 million+ refugees, multiple famine zones, and drone strikes increasingly targeting civilian areas, Sudan's humanitarian trajectory is the most acute in the current monitoring window — and remains structurally underreported relative to its severity.

In Russia-Ukraine, the post-Easter resumption of mass strikes — killing at least 58 civilians across multiple oblasts in the week of 11–17 April — confirms ACLED's structural assessment that Russia is using intense strike bursts to weaken Kyiv's negotiating position and internal coherence ahead of summer diplomatic pressure. Territorial advances in Sumy, Kharkiv, Donetsk, and Zaporizhia continue at a grinding pace. The Easter ceasefire is assessed as a tactical Russian weapons conservation pause consistent with the 2025 pattern, not a diplomatic signal. Ukraine's March 2026 drone swarm on Moscow (250–300 drones in a single night) qualifies as a swarm doctrine threshold event and represents the most significant Ukrainian escalatory action of the current cycle, though it has not altered the fundamental front-line trajectory.

In Gaza, Israel's control of over 75% of territory and the Hamas disarmament standoff represent a structural stalemate with no viable end-state framework. The Board of Peace's disarmament demand, absent political security guarantees for Hamas, is assessed by Crisis Group as insufficient to produce compliance. The humanitarian situation — population confined to under 25% of territory, aid subject to Israeli discretion, no reconstruction pathway — meets the threshold for humanitarian purgatory as described by ICG. The Iran theatre has seen a tactical improvement with the US-Iran two-week ceasefire, but ACLED's own analysts assess Iran as more likely to continue fighting than concede, and the ceasefire's two-week timeframe signals a pause rather than structural de-escalation. Iran's missile stockpile has been materially degraded, which may constrain near-term escalation capacity but does not resolve the underlying conflict drivers.

Myanmar and DRC remain structurally under-covered this cycle due to search constraint limitations — a coverage failure per schema Rule 3. Myanmar continues as the world's most fragmented conflict (1,200+ armed groups); DRC's eastern theatre remains active around the Rubaya coltan mine with Rwandan proxy dynamics unresolved. Haiti's gang crisis has driven a near-doubling of fatalities to 4,500+ killed, elevating it to the 8th most severe conflict globally. The Sahel arc is worsening with confirmed Sudan-Chad spillover and JNIM internal fragmentation driving a central Sahel crackdown.

In the coming 7 days, watch for: (1) whether the US-Iran ceasefire holds beyond its two-week window or collapses into renewed missile exchange; (2) RSF-SPLM-N progress toward Damazin — capture of a Blue Nile state capital would represent a major escalation threshold; (3) any SAF response to Blue Nile advances that draws in Ethiopian border dynamics; (4) whether Hamas's disarmament negotiations produce any framework signal or collapse entirely; (5) Russian strike tempo in Ukraine as a leading indicator of whether Moscow is preparing a summer offensive or consolidating for diplomatic positioning.

open_findings.headline: South Sudan has returned to civil war as President Kiir abandoned the 2018 peace agreement, with RSF-SPLM-N advances raising suspicion of Sudanese army proxy support for rebels — creating a direct Sudan-South Sudan conflict linkage.
open_findings.why_it_matters: South Sudan's return to warfare introduces a new regional conflict node directly adjacent to Sudan's active war, with Uganda backing Kiir and suspected SAF support for rebels creating a proxy war dynamic that could rapidly escalate into a multi-state confrontation and generate mass cross-border displacement into an already saturated regional humanitarian system.

open_findings.headline: ACLED documents that 60 days of the Iran war allowed violence, unrest, and instability to escalate across six other conflicts largely out of view — a documented attention displacement effect with structural monitoring implications.
open_findings.why_it_matters: The Iran war's dominance of global conflict attention [TRUNCATED — token limit]
